title: The Catalogue Map
description: Start at the top-level README, the catalogue's own explanation of itself. It lays out the layered directory structure (a shared common/ core plus one directory per language), the two install methods, the rules-vs-skills distinction, and the rule-priority model where language-specific rules override common rules like CSS specificity. Read this first and the rest of the tour becomes a guided walk through the structure it describes.
languageLesson: These are 'rules' files — always-on guardrails written in Markdown with YAML frontmatter whose path globs scope each rule to the files it governs. Unlike on-demand skills, rules are loaded as standing context, so the catalogue is organized to keep each rule small and concern-focused.

title: The Universal Core
description: The common/ directory is the foundation every language inherits. These four files are the most depended-upon nodes in the entire catalogue: common/coding-style.md (immutability, KISS/DRY/YAGNI, naming), common/patterns.md (Repository pattern, consistent API responses), common/security.md (pre-commit checks, secret management, incident response), and common/testing.md (80% coverage, three test tiers, red-green-refactor TDD). Every per-language rule file points back to one of these as its baseline — understand them and you understand the default that all 20+ languages specialize.

title: ECC's Working Philosophy
description: Beyond the per-concern rules, common/ holds the meta-files that encode how ECC expects an agent to work. development-workflow.md defines the research-and-reuse-first, then plan-TDD-review pipeline; code-review.md gates code before merge with a checklist and severity levels; agents.md catalogues the specialized subagents and when to delegate or run them in parallel; hooks.md introduces the PreToolUse/PostToolUse/Stop automation model. Together these reveal the catalogue's agent-first, test-driven worldview — the 'why' behind the concern-specific rules in Step 2.

title: How a Language Extends the Core
description: Now watch the extension mechanism in action with a full language vertical. TypeScript is the ideal first example because it is itself a foundation that React, Angular, Vue, and Nuxt build on. Each file here extends its common/ counterpart via a 'related' edge: typescript/coding-style.md adds explicit public-API typing and Zod validation on top of the universal style rules, while patterns, security, and testing each layer language-specific guidance over the shared baseline from Step 2. This four-way split by concern (style / patterns / security / testing) is the template every language directory repeats.
languageLesson: TypeScript generics express reusable, type-parameterized contracts — ApiResponse<T>, Repository<T>, useDebounce<T> — and the ruleset prefers `unknown` narrowing over `any`, string-literal unions over enums, and `z.infer` to derive types from Zod schemas so validation and types stay in lockstep.

title: When a Language Overrides the Default
description: Extension usually means 'add to' the common baseline, but the precedence model also allows a language to override it outright — the clearest illustration of the CSS-specificity analogy from Step 1. golang/coding-style.md deliberately diverges from the universal style guidance: where common/ favors generic immutability, Go mandates 'accept interfaces, return concrete structs', small 1-3 method interfaces, and %w error wrapping. The language-specific rule wins. Compare it side by side with common/coding-style.md to feel exactly where the override lands.
languageLesson: Go idioms drive the override: gofmt/goimports formatting is non-negotiable, interfaces are defined by the consumer not the producer, and errors are wrapped with %w so callers can unwrap with errors.Is/errors.As — a pattern that has no analogue in the language-agnostic baseline.

title: A Two-Level Stack
description: Some rulesets sit two layers deep. React does not extend common/ directly for everything — it extends typescript/ (Step 4), which in turn extends common/ (Step 2). This is the catalogue's most tightly-coupled cluster: react/patterns.md and react/hooks.md cross-reference each other AND both reach back through typescript/patterns.md to common/patterns.md. The result is a layered stack — universal principles, then the TypeScript substrate, then React's component, RSC-boundary, and hooks-discipline specializations on top. Trace the 'related' edges here to see inheritance compose across three levels.
languageLesson: Note the filename collision: react/hooks.md is about React's Rules of Hooks (useEffect, useMemo, the stale-closure trap), NOT the Claude Code PostToolUse/Stop hooks runtime that every other directory's hooks.md describes. The leading blockquote in the file disambiguates the two meanings.

title: Breadth: Systems, Mobile, and Frameworks
description: With the mechanism understood, sample how it scales across very different ecosystems. rust/patterns.md leans on traits, newtypes, and enum state machines for a systems language; swift/patterns.md applies protocol-oriented design, value types, and actors for Apple-platform mobile; and python/fastapi.md shows a third pattern — a framework-specific ruleset that extends python/patterns.md and python/security.md rather than common/ directly. Each is the same concern-split template from Step 4, re-skinned for its language's idioms, confirming the structure is uniform even as the content diverges wildly.

title: The Web Domain's Extra Concerns
description: Not every directory uses the standard four-file split. The web/ domain is a cross-cutting substrate beneath the UI frameworks that adds two concerns the others lack: web/design-quality.md enforces an anti-template policy with banned patterns and a per-component review checklist, and web/performance.md sets Core Web Vitals targets and bundle budgets. These sit alongside the usual style/security/testing files and feed React, Angular, Vue, and Nuxt — showing the catalogue extends its own template when a domain demands it.

title: The Recurring Cross-Language Triad
description: Close by zooming back out to the pattern that holds the whole catalogue together. Look at common/security.md, common/testing.md, and common/hooks.md as a triad — every single language directory carries its own security.md, testing.md, and hooks.md that extends these three. They are consistent cross-language checklists: security hardening, the testing/coverage contract, and the tooling-automation hooks that fire on edits. Whatever language you arrive in, you can predict these three files exist and know roughly what they enforce — the uniformity that makes the catalogue navigable is itself the final lesson.
